content:
{"notes": [{"url": "https://nsidc.org/learn/parts-cryosphere/frozen-ground-permafrost/why-frozen-ground-matters", "title": "Why Frozen Ground Matters", "summary": "Frozen Ground & Permafrost Why it Matters Since permafrost contains lots of organic matter like frozen plants and, occasionally, animals within, it holds an enormous amount of carbon. The Arctic contains nearly one-third of Earth's stored soil carbon in frozen ground and permafrost. If it thaws, microbial activity will lead to a release of heat-trapping greenhouse gases, carbon dioxide and methane, to the atmosphere.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["As of 2020, estimates suggest as much as 2.5 times more carbon is locked within permafrost than there is in the global atmosphere.", "Permafrost also lies beneath the Arctic Ocean, known as submarine or subsea permafrost, where vast reserves of geological methane are hibernating in an icy form known as methane clathrates.", "Although permafrost can be thousands of years old, it is sometimes newly formed or about to thaw, and it often exists close to the melting point, making it particularly sensitive to even minor increases in temperature.", "Carbon sink or source Plants consume carbon dioxide through photosynthesis.", "But unlike model projections, a survey conducted in 2016 of 98 permafrost-region experts concluded that plants will not come to the rescue."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: As of 2020, estimates suggest as much as 2.5 times more carbon is locked within permafrost than there is in the global a"}, {"url": "https://earth.org/arctic-permafrost-abrupt-thaw-a-risk/", "title": "Arctic Permafrost Moving Toward Crisis, Abrupt Thaw a ...", "summary": "The cracked, buckling road between Fairbanks and Fox, Alaska, wends its way past snowy forests where slender birches and black spruce trees protrude out of the ground at impossible angles, slanting and swaying as if the very earth were alive. Scientists call these ‘drunken forests’. The ground here consists of permafrost — soil, sediment, rock and ice that often remains at or below 0 degrees Celsius year-round.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["The ground here consists of permafrost — soil, sediment, rock and ice that often remains at or below 0 degrees Celsius year-round.", "Permafrost covers approximately 22.8 million square kilometres in the Arctic, sub-Arctic and alpine regions — comprising nearly a quarter of the exposed land surface in the northern hemisphere. — In Alaska, 85% of the state lies within the permafrost", "Scientists began seriously worrying about climate change impacts on permafrost about 15 years ago, but not only because of degraded infrastructure.", "An estimated 1 400 gigatons of carbon — made up of decomposed plants and animals which once inhabited the Earth — can be found embedded in permafrost.", "By comparison, our atmosphere presently contains just 850 gigatons of carbon."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: The ground here consists of permafrost — soil, sediment, rock and ice that often remains at or below 0 degrees Celsius y"}, {"url": "https://research.fs.usda.gov/treesearch/53006", "title": "Long-term CO2 production following permafrost thawing", "summary": "Sigsgaard | | Year: | 2013 | | Type: | Scientific Journal | | Station: | Pacific Northwest Research Station | Abstract Thawing permafrost represents a poorly understood feedback mechanism of climate change in the Arctic, but with a potential impact owing to stored carbon being mobilized1–5. We have quantified the long-term loss of carbon (C) from thawing permafrost in Northeast Greenland from 1996 to 2008 by combining repeated sediment sampling to assess changes in C stock and >12 years of CO2 production in incubated permafrost samples. Field observations show that the activelayer thickness has increased by >1 cm yr-1 but thawing has not resulted in a detectable decline in C stocks.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Sigsgaard | | Year: | 2013 | | Type: | Scientific Journal | | Station: | Pacific Northwest Research Station | Abstract Thawing permafrost represents a poorly understood feedback mechanism of climate change in the Arctic, but with a potential impact o", "We have quantified the long-term loss of carbon (C) from thawing permafrost in Northeast Greenland from 1996 to 2008 by combining repeated sediment sampling to assess changes in C stock and >12 years of CO2 production in incubated permafrost samples.", "Field observations show that the activelayer thickness has increased by >1 cm yr-1 but thawing has not resulted in a detectable decline in C stocks.", "Laboratory mineralization rates at 5 C resulted in a C loss between 9 and 75%, depending on drainage, highlighting the potential of fast mobilization of permafrost C under aerobic conditions, but also that C at near-saturated conditions may remain la", "This is confirmed by a three-pool C dynamics model that projects a potential C loss between 13 and 77% for 50 years of incubation at 5 C."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Sigsgaard | | Year: | 2013 | | Type: | Scientific Journal | | Station: | Pacific Northwest Research Station | Abstract T"}, {"url": "https://climate.esa.int/en/news-events/long-term-permafrost-record-details-arctic-thaw/", "title": "Long-term permafrost record details Arctic thaw", "summary": "Frozen Arctic soils are set to release vast amounts of greenhouse gases to the atmosphere as they continue to thaw in coming decades. Despite concerns that this will fuel future global warming, the scale and speed of this important climate process remain uncertain. To help address this knowledge gap, ESA-funded researchers have developed and released a new permafrost dataset – the longest, satellite-derived permafrost record currently available.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["The new 21-year satellite-derived record details the annual changes to the northern hemisphere permafrost soils from 1997—2018.", "Centre for Environmental Data Analysis, 02 November 2020. doi:10.5285/28e889210f884b469d7168fde4b4e54f. http://dx.doi.org/10.5285/28e889210f884b469d7168fde4b4e54f"], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: The new 21-year satellite-derived record details the annual changes to the northern hemisphere permafrost soils from 199"}, {"url": "https://www.nature.com/articles/s43247-026-03189-5", "title": "Permafrost and wildfire carbon emissions indicate need for ... - Nature", "summary": "Abstract Rapid Arctic warming is thawing carbon-rich permafrost, releasing greenhouse gases that accelerate climate change. Despite the importance of this feedback, permafrost-enabled global-scale models simulate only gradual, top-down thickening of the seasonally-thawed soil. This ignores abrupt permafrost thaw and intensifying fire regimes that combust soil carbon and further accelerate thaw.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["Our model suggests that including permafrost thaw and fire-related carbon emissions reduces the remaining allowable carbon budgets from 2025 onward by 25 % ± 12 % for avoiding 1.5 °C and 17 % ± 7 % for avoiding 2.0 °C, relative to simulations without", "This ‘gradual thaw’ is the most widespread mechanism of permafrost degradation and is expected to result in the loss of 24–69% of near-surface permafrost by 210013,14,15,16,17.", "Notable among these are thermokarst (hereafter used interchangeably with ‘abrupt thaw’) processes, in which the thawing of permafrost that contains excess ground ice results in uneven subsidence or even collapse of the ground surface and subsequent e", "However, in the northern boreal and tundra regions, as much as 80–90% of emissions from combustion come from below-ground material32,33, meaning global-scale models tend to underestimate fire emissions across the tundra and boreal biomes by roughly a", "Results Emissions from permafrost thaw Previous work using a variety of future warming scenarios and representations of permafrost processes has provided a considerable range of estimates of cumulative permafrost emissions this century from gradual t"], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: Our model suggests that including permafrost thaw and fire-related carbon emissions reduces the remaining allowable carb"}, {"url": "https://lamont.columbia.edu/news/permafrost-emissions-must-be-factored-global-climate-targets-says-study", "title": "Permafrost Emissions Must Be Factored Into Global Climate Targets ...", "summary": "Permafrost Emissions Must Be Factored Into Global Climate Targets, Says Study As the Arctic melts, permafrost there has the potential to send huge amounts of carbon into the atmosphere, but exactly how much is up for grabs, depending on what we do to stem climate change in coming years. By the end of this century, permafrost in the rapidly warming Arctic will likely emit as much carbon dioxide and methane into the atmosphere as a large industrial nation, and over time potentially more than the United States has emitted since the start of the Industrial Revolution. But that’s only one possible future for the vast stores of carbon locked in formerly perennially frozen but now-thawing ground in the Arctic.", "perspectives": ["Scientific"], "key_facts": ["The team estimates that under a low warming scenario—one that could be achieved if the global community limited warming to 2 degrees C or below by reducing fossil fuel emissions—permafrost would release 55 billion metric tons of greenhouse gases by t", "This forms lakes or causes other changes to surface hydrology.", "Once formerly frozen ground erodes or subsides, the carbon stored there can enter the atmosphere via microbial respiration.", "A 300-400 word deep analysis focused on findings relevant to the research question, including:
   - Key data points, statistics, and numerical findings
   - Methodology or approach described (if any)
   - Contradictions or tensions with commonly know", "Which STORM perspectives are covered (from: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends)
3. 3-5 specific facts with numbers, dates, or named entities
4."], "knowledge_contribution": "Evidence: The team estimates that under a low warming scenario—one that could be achieved if the global community limited warming "}]}
